speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five. Survivor Morris Kesselman, K-E-S-S-E-L-M-A-N. May 1, 1996. You were telling us that you received a letter from the cousins asking you why you were getting married in Germany, and what did you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them I found a, a nice girl, and I'd like to get married. I can't live like this. I don't know what's going to happen to us, and I-- when am I going to-- where am I going to wind up? We didn't know we're going to be able to come to the United States or any place else. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got married, and then I never, never received anymore letters, not knowing why. I found out later that she died when I came to the States looking for her. That's the reason I didn't get any more letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you didn't want to have any children right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- we didn't have a place too much. We had one room and didn't work. We just got rationed so much food. And we didn't want to bring up in Germany our child. To tell you the truth, I didn't want my child to be born in Germany. But it happened. And another thing is if a girl got married, and she didn't have a child, they thought that she was castrated, that the ovaries were taken out and she can't have any children. So everybody became pregnant. And Rita is not pregnant. And everybody said, don't worry. You'll become pregnant. So when she became pregnant, she's sort of happy too, not knowing whether we want a child, but she was very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that there was the fear that someone had been castrated, or ovaries taken out. Was that done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was done. I was taken for that, and they never got to me. I still have three friends in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They actually castrated them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who castrated them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. The SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this done?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz. Not in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they do that? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what's the reason it is, but that they did study what's going to happen to those people or whatever. There's three of my friends still live in New York which were castrated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was taken part of the group, but didn't get to me. They sent me back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? What happened to you that you weren't taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Too many of them that particular day. I don't know. We actually didn't know when we were taken where we're going, what for.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a little surprise. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. One is Stopat. The other one is Yanek. He lives in Texas. And one, Avrum, is a baker. I mean, let me say this to you. Anybody from the survivors is castrated we know. If I don't know, somebody else knows. Not a hidden thing. Can't have any children. One just died in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A terrible thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel then, when you had your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very good about it. I mean, the fact is, we had a little thing go between me and my wife. If we have a girl, I said that you can have your mother's name. We had names to go around. She says well, if we have a boy, you can have your father's name. Then we had a boy. I says, you know, Rita, you can still have your mother's name, whatever you want, or your father's name. She said, no, deal was a deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you name him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We named him Osher. His name was Arthur now.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to leave Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if my wife told you, my wife had a cousin here. They were farmers. And we got a letter from Israel when we couldn't go to Israel from another cousin to write them. This the address. And they actually made our papers because, having a farm, I could have come in as a farmer, and I wouldn't have to wait for a quota. And it was in the making. But then, the Truman refugee act opened up, that so many hundreds of thousands of refugees can come in from the concentration camps. And we were able to come in faster under Truman and Truman refugee act. We signed up, and we were sent to a doctor to check if we are healthy. And we finally receive papers when my son was about 10 months that we are going to be going to the United States June, this and this date. We were very happy about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to change your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I changed my name because I had family in the United States by Kesselman. And, you know, people came to me and said, you know, if somebody has a relative United States, has a better way of coming to the States. So why don't you change your name? It was legal to change your name. So I went into the German city hall, and I changed my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in the United States? When we came in, we lived in Boston, the section of Roxbury. It was a Jewish section, poor neighborhood. I was only making $14 a week, and we were paying $20 a month rent. And we lived there for about three years with one child. When another child was born 23 months later, it wasn't easy. We didn't want that child either to happen. But it happened, and somehow we made do. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Allen, and Jewish name Efraim. This was my mother's-- my wife's father's name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In fact, in this country, we will be able to give him two names. It was a-- I don't know. Everybody says, give him two names. In the old country, very seldom somebody had two names. So my older son, his name is only Arthur, for my father's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is Allen's second name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allen has two names. Efraim is after the father's name, and after the mother's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, funny, when I came to this country, I was only-- this was 1949, so I was 23 years old-- 22. And they asked me, the Jewish agency, which to try to help us, what kind of profession we have? I says, I had no profession. I never worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said to me, what was your father's profession? I say, he was in textile business. So they gave me a textile job. And I happened to go to a textile job. In those days, it was the minimum wage, $0.40. And I was hired.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those people were very, very good. Although, I didn't have nothing to do. They didn't have no job for me, but they hired me. It was no money. I couldn't get by, took a little while. And then the Korea war broke out, and they got government contracts. And I was drafted into the war. [LAUGHS] But because I had two children, and I worked in a military plant, I had a letter, and I was given a deferment. And eight months later, I was the supervisor-- assistant supervisor, at this plant. And I made a little more money. Then I became a supervisor, then I became superintendent. And I was nine years in that plant. And nine years later, I went into my own business, not that I had money to go into. But by coincidence, the two partners split up, and I went with one of the partners, and I became a partner. He put a little money in. And I was in business 34 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you retired now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired in 1988 when I was 63 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We have one more son, Bobby, which was born 1958. It's sort of a surprise, but he brought us a lot of luck. When he was born, I went into the business. He's married. He's got a lovely wife and a child, very educated, happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your other sons married?

speaker: interviewee
text: All three sons of mine married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the names of the daughter-in-laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The oldest one is Leslie, the middle one is Moreen, and the third one is Jill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have the oldest grandson, which he's in college now, the third year. His name is David. He's 21 years old. The second grandchild is Arielle. She's 19. She's also in college in Brandeis, is 19 years old. And we have a 15 year-- two 15-year-olds. One from one son, my oldest, is Todd, and from my middle boy is Justin. And then we have a 13-year-old. His name is Derek. And we have Jarrod, a nine-year-old boy. And we have one little girl which is one-year-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sons have bar mitzvahs?

speaker: interviewee
text: All my sons had bar mitzvahs, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel at your first son's bar mitzvah, when you did not have one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really great. I, I'll tell you what. When I came to this country, I didn't believe in any religion. Nothing was for me, although my wife never gave up. I never went to work the high holidays, but I never went to the temple either. But having sons, I felt that they should get a chance to, to pick if they want it or not to want it, and I came back to it. And I became observant as much as I could. I worked Saturdays in the early days. But in the last 20 years, I haven't worked Saturdays. Not because I was that observant, but I could afford not to go to work. And when I didn't work, I went to temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy your son's bar mitzvahs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so, yeah. Yeah. We had a lot of friends by then, and we had 200 people. They were very, very nice bar mitzvahs, all my three sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have any dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: There isn't a night that something doesn't come into my head. Many nights, like take last week, when we watched the shtetl. I didn't sleep all night. I went up. I went out of bed. My wife says, what are you doing? I said, I'm going to go to the other bedroom and watch television. It comes back then. If it isn't for one reason, another reason. And the worst part of it, when survivors get together, no matter for what reason, no matter for good or bad, somehow, when we end up, we were back in camp. And it's a big, big-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] troubling in our minds, in our hearts. Can never forget. You can forgive, and you can rationalize, but nobody lets you forget, even if you wanted to. There's always something-- a newspaper, or some swastika, some temple being disturbed that reminds you all of those things what you went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect do you think-- those years that you went through during the war, what effect has that had on the way you brought up your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you a thought. We gave more than we could. Like any other parents, we tried our kids to have. But the biggest thing was education. I tell you one little fast story. I hardly made any money, and I bought two policies for $0.50 a week to send my two older boys to college. My younger one wasn't born. Not knowing what it means, what's going to be. I lost it three, four times. I didn't have the $0.50. But somehow, I kept it back, and would you believe, after 20 years, I got $500. Not knowing what the $500 could do for that. But it was so in us that our kids should have an education, that I felt without education, you not going to get any place. And thank God our kids are very educated, professionals. And we tried the best we could. And this, I try to put my-- distill in my grandchildren that they should go to college, you know, this. And I hope they will follow. But it's my son's job now, but we tried to give a push here and there if we can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been active in Holocaust Memorial organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, very much so. I was president for six years in greater Boston. And I was involved in several monuments at Brandeis University also American Gathering and Washington museum. And until this day, we offer charity. We buy bonds. We have bond drives, We, we're very involved. We have involved a hospital in Israel. Very involved. Because, again, I wanted to, and thank God that, financially, we could do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's now 50 years since the end of the war. What message would you like to give to your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I like to my message to give to my children and grandchildren, to live a nice life, and look for the future, and to love each other, and to carry on our traditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many people did not survive. Why do you think that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked myself many, many times the same thing. I really don't know. There is no answer to it. It was pure luck. Because you couldn't have it-- from one day to the next day, you didn't know if you're going to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hope that the world learned a lesson and stop the killing what's going on all over the world. The people should live with each other a little more better, and respect each other. And I hope I'll see it on my lifetime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is myself, Morris Kesselman. It was taken in 1945 after the liberation. This was one of my-- one of my first pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Feldafing, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Marburg, Germany in 1945. Second from the right is a friend of mine in camp who is Stopat. Fourth from the right is-- was my future wife, and fifth to my right is myself. And on the bottom, Yanek is second from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture from the right was taken in Germany August-- September 1945, when I worked in the PX. And the second picture is my future wife and her girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same time, August or September, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Marburg, Germany. This is one of my wedding pictures. It was taken on August 27, 1945 in Sulzheim, Germany, and with all my friends around me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my son's wedding in 1976, myself and my three associates in business. And the four wives stayed behind us. New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken two years ago in Kendall on a Pasech night. This is my only surviving cousin, which sits in the middle of the picture, from 75 to 80 relatives. On the right is Mr. Grazilsky and his wife, and from the left is Rita Morris Kesselman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kendall.

speaker: interviewer
text: What state?

speaker: interviewee
text: Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture represents an unveiling of a monument of the Holocaust survivors in Brandeis University, 1991 in August, before Yom Tov. Myself held a speech in unveiling the monument.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a monument with a statue of Job, which is now in Brandeis University for the last 40 years. But it was moved to one place to another place, and added tablets of concentration camp, which are described in Yad Vashem, on the memorial in Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture what was taken in 1989 in Fontainebleu, Miami, Florida. In the center is myself, my wife, which was given to us [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a tablet for inscription and for having raised money for a bone drive. To the left of myself is the president from American Gathering, Meed, Mr. Meed. From the left is [INAUDIBLE]. From the right is the Prime Minister of Israel, Rabin, and one of his aids, a general.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's on this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a wedding picture of my youngest son Bob, which is in the center with his wife, Jill. From the right is one of my grandsons, is Derek, then Todd and David. Standing up is my son Arthur. On the left is my son Allen. And, and from him to the left is Arielle, then is Justin. Sitting down is Moreen, one of my daughter-in-laws, and Jarrod the son on the knees, myself and my wife, and my daughter-in-law, Leslie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1996. It's my youngest grandchild from my son Bob and Jill. And her name is Leah, and she was named after my older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamden, Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Morris, could you introduce your wife, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife Rita, which I love her very much. We are married for 50 years, coming, and I hope we stay together for a long time and enjoy each other and our children and our grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rita, would you like to say something to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Morris, you're the best thing that happened to me, and I hope we'll have many more years together. This is the best time in our lives now. Our children are married, we are-- and now a vacation home. We're enjoying life together. When you were younger, you didn't have time. You tried to make our lives better. But now, we can enjoy each other's company, and this is like our honeymoon now. I hope we'll have many good years together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
